title: Inference Capacity Staircase & Rollbacks Playbook
preview:
Header:
Date: 2026-03-18
Start: 11:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Mateo Alvarez, Priya Nair, Liam O'Connor, Jordan Blake, Elena Park, Noah Wright, Sana Mir, Greg Foster

Summary:
Quick internal workshop to align on a concrete 'capacity staircase' (phased ramps), explicit rollback criteria for model and capacity regressions, and a short playbook for dry-run/drill execution. Focus was on launch readiness for the next private deployment wave and a plan to avoid cost surprises while keeping SLOs.

Topics:
- Capacity staircase definition (levels, triggers)
- Canary and dry-run plan
- Rollback criteria: quality regressions vs infra regressions
- Observability needs and runbook edits
- Security/audit notes for private deployments

Action items (captured live): see metadata section above.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava: alright, uh thanks everyone for squeezing this in, I know the calendars are tight. the goal is to leave with a concrete, like, staircase thresholds doc and the first dry-run scheduled.
[00:18] Elena: yep, from exec side want clear guardrails — if latency crosses 220ms p95 for more than 5min we need an automatic step down, or at least an alert that triggers rollback.
[00:30] Noah: I think 220 as a hard number might be tough on the 8k contexts, we should scope by model size. maybe per-model thresholds, and then a global SLO budget.
[00:45] Mateo: agreed. we have telemetry split by model+region already but we need a staircase with 3 levels: baseline, stress, and surge. baseline is normal autoscale, stress is reserved burst, surge is emergency cap (manual). each level has triggers: latency, error rate, token throughput.
[01:05] Priya: can we call them v1/v2/v3? the names matter less than the actions — for v2 the plan is increase batch window slightly and enable KV caching for prefixes, for v3 we should auto-fallback to a quantized variant or smaller model.
[01:25] Liam: caution on auto-fallback — there's user-affecting quality regressions. we need rollback criteria both for infra failures and for quality regressions. that means an observable that's not just latency: e.g. eval prompt set failure rate or hallucination proxy.
[01:40] Sana: on the security side, any autoscaling or rollbacks in private deployments must be logged with the instance id and KMS key usage. audit trail is mandatory for some customers. also, VPC egress events need to be captured.
[02:00] Jordan: ok capturing, also note that cost must be part of the staircase. if burst > X token-minutes we trigger a cost review window and possibly step-down. otherwise finance will freak.
[02:15] Greg: for dry-run we should pick a low-traffic weekend slot and run a canary that ramps traffic using replay plus synthetic tails to exercise long-context paths. the sim should include KV cache warmup so we don't get skewed results.
[02:35] Ava: agreed — Mateo you own the sim plan?
[02:40] Mateo: yep, i'll do a 4-hour sim with three ramps, I'll include latency, p95/p99, tail lat, token throughput, and error rate. spreadsheet will have columns for thresholds and pass/fail criteria.
[02:55] Elena: what's the rollback mechanism we're comfortable with? full traffic cut or model variant fallback?
[03:05] Priya: prefer staged: first fallback model variant (quantized), if QA metric drops below threshold then step to capacity step-down (move traffic to smaller region or dedicated pool), last resort cut traffic. incorporate vote from oncall.
[03:25] Liam: that needs clear criteria: e.g., quality proxy > 2% fail on eval set OR latency p99 > 500ms for 10 minutes leads to vote. we should automate the vote if it's infra-exit (OOMs, GPU errors).
[03:45] Noah: on capacity accounting: the staircase should expose reserved burst buckets per customer so we can bill and also stop accidental overrun. we should include a strap on the console that shows current staircase stage and remaining burst minutes.
[04:05] Priya: we talked about KV cache being mis-heard in prior calls as 'KV cash' — note: caching warms cost but helps latency; we'll measure cost per token with cache on/off.
[04:20] Jordan: heh yep, recorded.
[04:25] Ava: operational playbook edits — Liam, can you propose amendments to runbook for staged rollback, including who has the unilateral authority to move to v2/v3?
[04:40] Liam: i'll propose that oncall can move to v2 (stress) but v3 (surge) requires approval from capacity lead + product (Noah + Elena). final traffic cut needs VP ok.
[04:55] Greg: and we'll add a checklist for the dry-run: prechecks (metrics sane), smoke test endpoints, KMS audit hook enabled, and roll-forward plan.
[05:10] Ava: timeline check: draft stairs doc by Monday (Ava), sim by Wednesday (Mateo), rollback hook prototype by next Friday (Priya), dry-run targeted end of next week (Greg).
[05:25] Elena: quick question — how do we handle private deployments where customers want full manual control? do we still enforce staircases?
[05:35] Sana: for privacy customers we ship the scaffolding; by default it's opt-in for automatic steps, but we still require audit logs and a manual approval path. the defaults will be conservative.
[05:50] Mateo: technical gotcha — when we do model variant fallback we need to ensure KV cache format compatibility or else cache miss storms will happen. we should add a cache compatibility check in the rollback hook.
[06:05] Priya: yep, that's on my prototype scope. i'll include a quick latency-safe migration that invalidates prefixes older than N seconds to avoid cold-miss avalanche.
[06:25] Jordan: can we add a short 'blast radius' diagram to the staircase doc that maps components affected (frontend, router, runtime, kv cache, billing)?
[06:40] Ava: good idea, Jordan — that'll help execs understand risk.
[06:50] (crosstalk)
[07:00] Ava: ok smaller notes: include specific SLO windows, escalation path, and the exact telemetry dashboards we'll need. Mateo, please list dashboard IDs so we can pair them to thresholds.
[07:18] Mateo: will do — i'm also going to include an 'emergency capacity token' concept that is separate from reserved burst and requires manual approval.
[07:30] Noah: one more ask — instrument the console to show last rollback reason: quality/infrastructure/cost. that's critical for postmortems.
[07:45] Priya: agreed. building that into trace logs is straightforward but we need UX work to surface it. I'll coordinate with Jordan for the wire.
[07:58] Ava: wrapping — any final blockers?
[08:05] Elena: none here. just want the doc to be crisp.
[08:10] Liam: need to validate SSO and audit hooks for private customers before dry-run. Sana, can you sign off once done?
[08:20] Sana: yes i'll sign off once I see KMS audit logs and VPC egress hooks in place.
[08:30] Ava: cool. recap next steps in chat and i'll own the doc.
[08:35] (meeting ends)

Post-call quick notes (auto):
- Some speech mis-recognition around 'KV cache' and 'canary sim' replaced with 'KV cash' and 'candy sim' in earlier auto transcript; corrected above.
- A few timestamps drift because of overlapping talkers; manual cleanup recommended if used for legal/audit purposes.

Recap (final):
- Deliverables: staircase thresholds doc (Ava), canary sim results spreadsheet (Mateo), rollback hook prototype (Priya), dry-run schedule and checklist (Greg), runbook edits (Liam).
- Dry-run target: weekend slot week of 2026-03-29.
- Acceptance criteria: per-model thresholds pass in sim, KV cache compatibility verified, security audit hooks present, and rollback behavior tested with postmortem template filled.

References shared during call:
- \"staircase-spec-v0\" doc link (to be attached in calendar)
- canary-spreadsheet template (sheet link)
- runbook PR template (repo link)

End of transcript.

title: Known-issues sprint publish review (workarounds + 'when to escalate')
preview: Internal publish-review for the Known-Issues Sprint. Team did a rapid pass over five workaround articles (streaming disconnects/timeouts, 429 quota mismatch, Dedicated autoscaling oscillation, Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations, audit log delivery latency) to ensure they are copy/paste-ready for Support, include clear escalation criteria, and avoid customer-sensitive details. Agreed on a consistent 'When to escalate' rubric mapped to Support P0/P1 triggers, added owners/reviewers per article, and aligned on what must be linked from Support macros and the known-issues index before merging docs PRs.

title: Support + SRE playbook alignment (escalation triggers, bridges, update cadence)
preview: Support leadership and SRE aligned on (1) what constitutes P0/P1 from Support vs Sev0/Sev1 in incident terms, (2) concrete paging/escalation triggers (error-rate/latency/customer-count/revenue risk), (3) Slack bridge conventions and when to move from #support bridge into #incidents, and (4) update cadence language for enterprise vs non-enterprise (and how to handle “Waiting on Customer” without gaming the SLA). The group agreed to standardize a short “page template” (request IDs, region, model, recent deploys, customer tier, and what changed), require linking the SUP Jira ticket in the bridge, and to use a consistent cadence phrased as targets rather than guarantees. Follow-ups include edits to the Confluence SLA table wording, Jira automation for required fields + SLA timers, and incident-bot improvements to pin SUP ticket links in bridge channels.

title: Marketplace onboarding walkthrough (internal dry run) — subscription → org provisioning → first key
preview: Internal walkthrough of the end-to-end marketplace onboarding flow to validate the UX and docs before submission. Team identified confusion around org linking for existing Redwood orgs, unclear messaging during entitlement propagation delays (AWS especially), and missing docs for the "already subscribed" and "multiple subscriptions" edge cases. Engineering confirmed expected entitlement API error codes and noted where console should map them to actionable retry guidance. Docs team flagged needed screenshots and a clearer separation of AWS vs GCP steps, plus a single canonical "How marketplace billing maps to usage" section to reduce support tickets.

Team aligned on primary activation KPIs (first successful request + first 100k tokens within 7 days), experiment tracks (email drip v2, in-console nudges, paid retargeting), instrumentation/event taxonomy, and guardrails (deliverability, CAN-SPAM/GDPR, sales opp exclusions, and experiment integrity). title: Regional failover automation upgrade - kickoff
preview: Kickoff for the Reliability Program workstream to upgrade regional failover automation across Hosted API and Dedicated. Team aligned on problem statement (manual/partial automation, noisy triggers, slow detection, hard to validate), in-scope components (signal pipeline, region health aggregation, controller + deterministic policy evaluation, guardrails/rollback, observability, runbooks, game days) and out-of-scope items (multi-cloud migration, serving runtime rewrite, SLA language). Open questions focused on trigger hierarchy (authoritative signals vs corroboration), anti-flap behavior, approval model (auto vs ack-required), and how to safely run controlled production failovers with limited blast radius.

title: Incident taxonomy working session (v1) - tricky classifications + owner mapping
preview: Working session to finalize incident taxonomy v1 edges and the owner mapping for disputed categories. Team aligned on keeping top-level types small (target ~12) with subtypes where needed. Resolved key disputes: capacity shortage remains Fleet primary; routing/fallback misbehavior remains Smart Routing primary; quota/rate limit enforcement is Control Plane primary (Support consulted for customer-caused throttling); streaming disconnects default to Edge/Gateway unless clear client SDK regression. Agreed to add a simple rule for multi-cause incidents: pick the mitigation owner as primary type, track other systems as contributing factors in postmortem. Observability reiterated cardinality guardrails (allowlist + unknown bucket). Action items captured for updating the decision tree, owner mapping config, and incident-bot suggestions.

title: Post-launch retro - Benchmark report campaign (KPI review + refresh plan)
preview: Team reviewed performance of the public benchmark report launch (landing page + interactive table + PDF + blog + webinar). Results were strong top-of-funnel with meaningful sales-assisted pipeline influence, but launch-day reliability issues (cache miss storm + 429s) created support load and some negative chatter. The methodology page and reproducibility artifacts were consistently cited as the trust driver. Next phase focuses on (1) tightening follow-up loops for high-intent leads, (2) shipping a lightweight “replicate this in your PoC” guide and a clearer metric explainer, (3) improving cache/CDN settings and guardrails for future spikes, and (4) establishing a refresh cadence with clear material-change thresholds and legal review gates.

[11:59] Noura El-Khoury: Works.

[12:01] Felix Schneider: One more: the tooltip on “latency/$” should define what the dollar is. It’s basically cost per output token in USD under the measured config. We should avoid confusion like “customer spend” vs “infrastructure cost.”

[12:16] Wei Chen: Yeah. We should say “estimated usage cost” or “usage cost at Redwood list price” depending on what it is.

[12:23] Jonas Weber: It is usage cost based on the pricing schedule for that deployment mode, yes.

[12:27] Soojin Lee: Then it’s “usage cost” not “infra cost.”

[12:31] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, I’ll put that in the action items: define tooltip text and confirm the pricing reference.

[12:38] Noura El-Khoury: Time check. We’re good. Now I want to do a quick run from the end: closing and follow-up. Soojin, your close.

[12:47] Soojin Lee: Thanks everyone. The report and interactive table are live, and you can download the PDF. If you’re building on Hosted, start with the models and regions that fit your latency and cost constraints and try it with your prompts. If you need predictable throughput or data isolation, we can help you size Dedicated. And if you have VPC or on-prem requirements, talk to us about Private.

[13:12] Ben Carter: Then I say: you’ll get a follow-up email with links, the recording, and a short “how we measured” explainer.

[13:18] Noura El-Khoury: Nice. Also include: “post questions in chat” and “we’ll publish an FAQ.”

[13:25] Wei Chen: We have that FAQ page drafted.

[13:28] Soojin Lee: Great.

[13:30] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, I think we’re done. I’ll read back the key action items.

[13:35] Noura El-Khoury: One, Felix: add tooltip/disclaimer next to latency slash dollar and link to methodology. Two, Wei: fix any “best/cheapest/across providers” language on landing page and confirm “usage cost” wording. Three, Alicia: update slide screenshots and bump disclaimer font for 720p readability. Four, Jonas and Claire: send me and Ethan a short methodology blurb we can repeat verbatim. Five, Ben: finalize follow-up emails and UTM tracking.

[14:05] Noura El-Khoury: Anything else before we stop recording?

[14:08] Ethan Wallace: Just one: I want a short line to address “you can reproduce it.” Like, “We’ll publish configs.”

[14:14] Claire Dubois: I can draft a sentence: “We publish the prompt mix, concurrency, warmup, and rerun rules; you can export the dataset and validate against your own workload.”

[14:24] Soojin Lee: Perfect.

[14:26] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Stopping.

(Recording ends)

Producer note: The call continued for a few minutes off-record to coordinate calendar holds and confirm rehearsal deck versioning.

title: Benchmark report kickoff — latency/$ economics (internal)
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Title: Benchmark report kickoff — latency/$ economics (internal)
Date: 2025-06-03
Time: 10:00–10:56 PT (recorded)
Duration: 56 min
Attendees (Redwood): Soojin Lee, Ethan Wallace, Ben Carter, Jonas Weber, Claire Dubois, Rina Kobayashi, Mateo Alvarez, Vanessa Ortiz, Neha Kapoor, Elliot Price, Wei Chen, Tyler Benson, Talia Benson

Summary (auto-generated):
- Team aligned on goals for a public benchmark report focused on production inference economics (latency per dollar) with reproducible methodology.
- Discussed scope: models/regions shortlist, workload design (prefill/decode mix), caching/batching policy, stability checks, and how to present variance.
- Confirmed launch assets: interactive web table, downloadable PDF, blog post, sales enablement kit, webinar.
- Identified legal/safe-claims constraints and the need for clear caveats vs competitor baselines.
- Assigned owners and immediate action items.

Topics (auto-generated):
1) What we’re publishing + why now
2) Methodology and fairness (workloads, prompt sets, caching/batching)
3) Regions, capacity reservations, run logistics
4) Messaging, disclaimers, and competitive language
5) Launch plan + funnel instrumentation

Action items (auto-generated):
- Share methodology v0.9 and prompt set proposal
- Draft capacity reservation plan
- Draft report outline and figure requirements
- Confirm artifact export + redaction requirements
- Create analytics event list and campaign tagging

Transcript:

[00:00] Soojin Lee: Alright, I think we’re at time. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the kickoff for the benchmark report—public-facing, focused on production inference economics, so like latency per dollar, not just raw tokens per second. Goal today is align on scope, owners, timeline, and like, what we will and won’t say.

[00:20] Talia Benson: Quick note, I’m recording and I’ll drop notes after. If anything sensitive comes up, call it out and we’ll flag for redaction.

[00:27] Ethan Wallace: Cool. And I’ll be annoying about narrative. Just warning everybody.

[00:31] Jonas Weber: Please be annoying, that’s helpful.

[00:34] Soojin Lee: Perfect. Let’s do a quick round of what each team needs out of this. I’ll start: PMM needs a credible, defensible story that’s consistent with what we can substantiate. Also Sales needs a talk track that doesn’t overclaim.

[00:48] Ben Carter: Growth perspective: we want this to drive signups and demo requests. So we need a landing page that converts, and instrumentation so we can attribute pipeline. Also, we should know what the “aha” is—like, what are we claiming Redwood is better at.

[01:02] Jonas Weber: Applied ML/perf side: we need methodology locked early. If we do “latency/$” it has to be well defined. Otherwise we’ll get roasted.

[01:11] Claire Dubois: Yup. And we need to make sure workloads reflect production distributions—prefill heavy vs decode heavy. Not just one synthetic prompt.

[01:18] Rina Kobayashi: From harness side: region sweeps are doable but capacity variability is real. We need reserved slots. Also we need to decide what artifacts are publishable—manifests, configs, etc.

[01:30] Mateo Alvarez: Runtime: we’re supportive, but I want to be clear: we are not going to ship kernel changes just to look good in a benchmark. Small guardrails or telemetry, fine. But not “benchmark-ware.”

[01:42] Neha Kapoor: Reliability: launch week traffic is a risk, plus we’ll get people hammering the page and maybe the API. So we need rate limit and caching plan.

[01:50] Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet: I need dates and region list. If you want NA, EU, APAC, I can try to reserve, but some regions are tight. We have to pick what’s realistic.

[02:01] Wei Chen: Docs/web copy: give me the disclaimers early. Also methodology summary needs to be readable, not a paper.

[02:09] Elliot Price: I’m here to help with the method write-up and make it consistent with how we talk about caching, batching, and what “typical” means.

[02:16] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s anchor on the “why now” and the deliverables. Ethan, can you summarize what’s shipping?

[02:24] Ethan Wallace: Sure. My current mental package is: 1) interactive table on the site—filter by model, region, context length maybe; 2) PDF report with narrative, charts, and a methodology section; 3) blog post that’s more digestible; 4) enablement kit—FAQ, talk track, maybe a one slide summary; and 5) webinar to walk through results and answer “is this fair.”

[02:47] Soojin Lee: Yes. And timeline wise, we’ve got like ~8 weeks in the strawman. We can adjust, but we should assume legal review and reviews will take time.

[03:00] Jonas Weber: Can I ask—are we planning any competitor baselines? Because that changes legal risk and methodology.

[03:06] Soojin Lee: Good question. Tentatively: we can include context, but we have to be cautious. We can say “here’s Redwood on X hardware with Y config.” If we do competitor numbers, it needs caveats and preferably from public sources or clearly stated measurement conditions.

[03:21] Ethan Wallace: I’d rather keep competitor references minimal in the first version. The story can be “this is what production economics look like; here’s how to evaluate.” But I know Sales will want comparison.

[03:34] Mateo Alvarez: Also competitor numbers are hard to make apples-to-apples. If someone runs AWS Bedrock with default settings vs our tuned runtime, people will argue.

[03:42] Claire Dubois: The Internet will argue regardless.

[03:44] (crosstalk)

[03:45] Ben Carter: If we don’t compare, do we still get pickup?

[03:48] Soojin Lee: We can still get pickup if the metric is novel and the methodology is strong. “Latency per dollar” and regional consistency is a good hook.

[03:56] Jonas Weber: On the technical side, we should publish enough artifacts so a skeptical engineer can reproduce. That’s the defense.

[04:06] Rina Kobayashi: +1, but we should plan redaction. Manifests can’t leak internal hostnames or request IDs.

[04:12] Wei Chen: And it has to be presented in a way that doesn’t scare off normal devs. Like “here’s the summary; here’s the full method if you want it.”

[04:21] Soojin Lee: Ok, let’s talk methodology. Claire, can you walk through what you think “latency/$” should be?

[04:28] Claire Dubois: Yeah. So, we need to define the unit: dollars per something and latency per something. Usually we can compute cost per 1M tokens given our pricing or underlying GPU cost for dedicated. But since this is public and spans hosted, maybe we compute using published hosted price per token. Then “latency/$” could be like (tokens per second) divided by ($ per second) effectively, but that gets confusing. Better: “time to generate a fixed output length” and “cost for that generation.” Then we can do “seconds per $” or invert to “outputs per $.”

[05:05] Jonas Weber: Right now internally we’ve been doing something like: latency per request (p50, p95) and cost per 1M tokens, and then a combined metric we call latency-per-dollar as a normalized score. But we need to define it precisely.

[05:18] Mateo Alvarez: Please no “score” that looks like we’re cooking numbers.

[05:22] Claire Dubois: Agree. We should avoid a single magic score unless it’s literally a ratio with clear units.

[05:28] Ben Carter: Marketing-wise, units are good. “p95 latency at $X per 1M tokens” is understandable.

[05:35] Ethan Wallace: Could be a table that toggles: show p50/p95 latency, throughput, and cost, then let people decide. Then we highlight a couple insights.

[05:46] Soojin Lee: Ok. Key methodology questions that keep coming up: caching/batching, concurrency, prompt sets, and warmups. Jonas, where are we right now?

[05:56] Jonas Weber: For a production-like benchmark, we should enable the things customers actually use. So continuous batching is on. For caching, it depends. Prefix caching can dramatically change economics for repeated prompts. If we enable it, we need to say under what cache hit rate. If we disable it, people will say we’re not showing real world savings.

[06:20] Elliot Price: I’ve seen this go sideways. We should separate: baseline run with cache off (to show raw serving performance) and an optional scenario with cache on with a defined synthetic hit rate or a defined workload that naturally has repeats.

[06:33] Claire Dubois: Another option: keep caching policy fixed—like “KV cache reuse off; standard attention KV cache obviously exists,” but no prefix reuse across requests. Then we can have a section: “if your workload has repeats, caching can improve cost/latency; here’s how to estimate.”

[06:51] Rina Kobayashi: That’s easiest to implement and defend. If we do cross-request caching, we need to model user behavior.

[06:58] Ben Carter: From funnel perspective, I’d rather not bury the lead in nuance, but I get it.

[07:05] Soojin Lee: Legal will also prefer less “it depends” but we have to be honest. We can message: “Benchmark uses conservative assumptions; your workload may see better results with caching.”

[07:17] Mateo Alvarez: Also, concurrency. If we run at low concurrency, we’ll look good on p50 but not throughput. If we run at high concurrency, we might degrade p95. We should pick something realistic.

[07:30] Claire Dubois: I propose we define multiple load points: concurrency 1 (single user), concurrency 8/16 (small production), and maybe 64 for stress. But that’s a lot of runs.

[07:43] Vanessa Ortiz: That is a lot of GPU hours.

[07:46] Jonas Weber: We can limit to two concurrencies: one “interactive” and one “service” mode. E.g., C=1 and C=16.

[07:55] Neha Kapoor: And we should run repeats across time windows to catch variance. Otherwise a region with a noisy neighbor day will look bad.

[08:05] Rina Kobayashi: We can add stability gates. Like if p95 variance > threshold across 3 runs, auto-rerun.

[08:13] Mateo Alvarez: That’s fine. But it adds time.

[08:16] Soojin Lee: Better time than a PR crisis.

[08:19] (laughter)

[08:22] Ethan Wallace: On prompt sets, what’s the plan? Because for narrative I need to say “we used a production mix.”

[08:29] Claire Dubois: We have an internal prompt set v2, but it’s a bit too “eval-y.” I want to update to something like v3 that includes: short chat turns, long context summarization, RAG-ish prompts, and structured output. Also include prefill-heavy tasks and decode-heavy tasks.

[08:50] Jonas Weber: And we should publish the categories and distributions, not necessarily the raw prompts if there’s any IP concerns.

[08:58] Wei Chen: If we don’t publish prompts, people may say it’s not reproducible.

[09:03] Elliot Price: We can publish hashed prompts or a synthetic open set. Or publish the generator code and category definitions.

[09:10] Rina Kobayashi: We can publish prompt IDs and lengths, and a small sample set. Full set maybe in a GitHub repo with review.

[09:20] Soojin Lee: Let’s park exact publication details, but align that we need reproducibility artifacts.

[09:26] Tyler Benson: Can I jump in on measurement outputs? For the web page, we need a stable schema. Model, region, context length bucket, concurrency, p50, p95, throughput, cost per 1M tokens, and a timestamp / run id. Also needs “methodology version.”

[09:44] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, we can do JSON + CSV export. But please decide early what dimensions we’re exposing because it affects data volume.

[09:53] Ben Carter: Also, for the interactive page, we want filters but not 20 knobs. Too many knobs hurts conversion.

[10:01] Ethan Wallace: Agree. We can default to the “recommended” workload and let advanced users toggle.

[10:06] Soojin Lee: Ok. Regions and capacity. Vanessa, can you outline constraints?

[10:12] Vanessa Ortiz: Sure. For NA we’re good: us-east and us-west are fine. For EU, eu-west is usually okay but we have some reserved commitments. APAC is the tricky one. ap-southeast sometimes has limited availability depending on instance type. If we want consistent results, we should reserve blocks and run all models in a tight window.

[10:34] Neha Kapoor: We also need to avoid running during known maintenance windows.

[10:38] Vanessa Ortiz: Right. And we need to choose instance types—like are we standardizing on one GPU class or are we “best available per region”? Because that affects fairness.

[10:50] Mateo Alvarez: For hosted, we control the fleet but it’s not always identical across regions. For a benchmark, maybe we pin to a specific GPU for all runs, even if it’s not our default in one region.

[11:03] Jonas Weber: If we change fleet config just for the benchmark, it’s not representative.

[11:08] Mateo Alvarez: Not change permanently, but reserve identical slices.

[11:12] Soojin Lee: This is one of those tradeoffs. We want representative but also comparable. Maybe we present “this is what customers see in region X as-of date,” and we list the hardware. That’s honest.

[11:28] Claire Dubois: Yes. Publish the hardware profile per region. Then no one can say we hid it.

[11:34] Ethan Wallace: But does that create confusion? “Why is region A faster? Because different GPUs.”

[11:40] Ben Carter: That might be okay. People care about what they will experience.

[11:45] Wei Chen: If we do that, we need a tooltip that explains “hardware may differ by region.”

[11:51] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s shift to messaging and claims. I know Legal isn’t on this call, but we should plan the review. The big risk: “fastest” claims. We should avoid absolutes unless we can substantiate across all workloads.

[12:09] Ethan Wallace: So the core narrative could be: 1) production inference economics are multi-dimensional; 2) Redwood provides a consistent, optimized runtime; 3) here’s what we measured and how; 4) key insights: regional consistency, p95 stability, and cost efficiency.

[12:28] Jonas Weber: Also include reliability. Not just speed. People care about tail latency.

[12:33] Neha Kapoor: Yes please. p95 and error rates. We can report 5xx/429 rates during runs maybe, or at least note we excluded failed runs.

[12:42] Claire Dubois: If we include error rates, we need to define exclusion criteria. Like if a run has more than X% errors, discard and rerun.

[12:50] Rina Kobayashi: We already have rerun logic we can add. Or manual gates.

[12:55] Soojin Lee: Ok. On competitive, we can say “compared to common managed endpoints, you should evaluate with these factors.” But we shouldn’t say “2x faster than OpenAI” unless we have very strong evidence and permission.

[13:10] Ben Carter: Can we at least mention “AWS Bedrock” as an example of “general managed endpoints”?

[13:15] Soojin Lee: Mentioning as category is okay. Specific numerical comparisons are where we need legal sign-off.

[13:22] Wei Chen: For disclaimers, we should include: results depend on workload, concurrency, region, time of day, and model version. Also “as of date.”

[13:34] Elliot Price: And “we used Redwood-optimized configs.” People will ask if they can get the same. We should say these settings are available to customers (batching, etc.) and we’ll link to docs.

[13:46] Mateo Alvarez: As long as that’s true. Some configs might be internal defaults.

[13:50] Jonas Weber: We should only publish numbers for configurations that customers can actually select or that are default behavior.

[13:57] Soojin Lee: Great constraint: publish only supported configs.

[14:01] Talia Benson: I’m noting: “supported vs experimental” needs definition. We should align with Product.

[14:07] Soojin Lee: Yes, Henry isn’t here but we’ll pull him in for model/region “supported” list.

[14:14] Ben Carter: On the funnel, I want to propose CTAs per segment. For self-serve, “Get API key + credits.” For enterprise, “Talk to us about Dedicated/Private.” The benchmark page should route to both.

[14:28] Ethan Wallace: And we need a “how we measured” explainer that’s short, so people don’t bounce.

[14:33] Wei Chen: I can draft that as a collapsible section.

[14:36] Tyler Benson: Analytics events I’ll need: filter changes, export clicks, PDF download, CTA clicks, time on page, and scroll depth maybe. Also UTM capture.

[14:49] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s discuss timeline. Strawman: next 2 weeks we lock methodology and implement any required instrumentation, weeks 3-5 run sweeps, week 6 content, week 7 reviews, week 8 launch. Is that feasible?

[15:07] Rina Kobayashi: Feasible if we don’t have a million workload variants.

[15:10] Vanessa Ortiz: Feasible if you give me region list this week.

[15:14] Jonas Weber: Feasible if methodology is frozen by end of next week. Otherwise it slips.

[15:20] Ethan Wallace: I need early data to draft charts. Even partial results.

[15:25] Claire Dubois: We can do an early “pilot sweep” with 2 models, 2 regions, to validate harness and produce initial charts.

[15:34] Mateo Alvarez: Good. That will also reveal runtime regressions.

[15:39] Neha Kapoor: And it helps SRE plan for launch week traffic—what endpoints, caching, etc.

[15:45] Soojin Lee: Ok, let’s list decisions we need this week.

[15:49] (pause)

[15:51] Soojin Lee: 1) Model shortlist. Jonas/Claire, can you propose? Keep it curated.

[15:58] Jonas Weber: We should include at least one small, one mid, one large model. Maybe a couple popular open weights. We should also consider embeddings? Or is it just generation?

[16:09] Ethan Wallace: For narrative, keep it to generation in v1, or it becomes too big.

[16:14] Ben Carter: Agree. Embeddings is a separate report.

[16:18] Claire Dubois: Ok, generation only. Proposed: a 7–8B class, a 13–14B class, a 70B-ish, and maybe one MoE if we support it. And we should pin model versions.

[16:33] Mateo Alvarez: Make sure they’re actually stable in production.

[16:36] Soojin Lee: 2) Region list: likely us-east, us-west, eu-west, and one APAC. Vanessa will advise.

[16:44] Vanessa Ortiz: I’d say ap-southeast if possible, or ap-northeast depending on capacity. I’ll confirm.

[16:51] Soojin Lee: 3) Workloads: at least two context length buckets, and two concurrencies. Claire to propose.

[16:58] Claire Dubois: Yep.

[17:00] Soojin Lee: 4) Caching stance: baseline without cross-request prefix caching; continuous batching on; publish that.

[17:08] Jonas Weber: Works. We should be explicit: we’re not modeling cache hit rates in the primary metric.

[17:14] Elliot Price: And we can add a sidebar: “If you have repeated prefixes, here’s how caching changes the math.”

[17:20] Soojin Lee: Great.

[17:22] (audio glitch)

[17:25] Ben Carter: One more: naming. Are we calling it “latency per dollar” or “latency/$” or “throughput per dollar”?

[17:33] Ethan Wallace: “Latency per dollar” is catchy but could be misunderstood. “Better latency at lower cost” is safer.

[17:41] Soojin Lee: We’ll test terms. But for now internally call it latency/$.

[17:47] Tyler Benson: If we’re using a combined metric, we need to decide if it’s ratio or two-axis plots.

[17:53] Jonas Weber: I’d prefer a frontier chart: latency vs cost. Then we can say “efficient frontier.”

[18:01] Ethan Wallace: Frontier chart is cool but maybe too nerdy. We can do both—frontier in PDF, table in web.

[18:10] Wei Chen: On web, keep it simple. Table + a couple charts.

[18:16] Soojin Lee: Ok. Let’s talk objections we anticipate. This will inform FAQ. Markus isn’t here, but we should seed it. What will engineers complain about?

[18:27] Jonas Weber: “Your workload isn’t my workload.”

[18:30] Claire Dubois: “You cherry-picked prompts.”

[18:33] Mateo Alvarez: “You tuned your runtime but not others.”

[18:36] Neha Kapoor: “Regions vary day to day.”

[18:39] Rina Kobayashi: “You hid errors and retries.”

[18:42] Ben Carter: “This doesn’t match what I see.”

[18:45] Soojin Lee: Great. So we need: clear methodology, versioning, run dates, hardware, and stability notes.

[18:55] Elliot Price: Also define how we treat streaming. Are we measuring time-to-first-token and time-to-last-token? People care.

[19:02] Claire Dubois: We should capture TTFT and TPOT maybe. But that multiplies metrics.

[19:09] Jonas Weber: At minimum we should report TTFT and end-to-end latency for a fixed token output. TTFT is key for chat.

[19:18] Mateo Alvarez: Streaming instrumentation exists; Omar can help, but not in this call.

[19:24] Soojin Lee: Let’s include TTFT + end-to-end p95 as core.

[19:29] Wei Chen: If we add TTFT, we need definitions on the page or people will misread.

[19:34] Ethan Wallace: That’s fine. We can do tooltips.

[19:38] Soojin Lee: Ok, I want to make sure we have clear owners. Jonas/Claire/Rina own benchmark design and data. Mateo/Neha/Vanessa own runtime+infra support and capacity. Ethan owns narrative. Ben+Tyler own funnel and analytics. Wei+Elliot own docs/methodology summary. I own the launch plan, message house, and sales enablement.

[20:04] Talia Benson: Captured.

[20:06] Soojin Lee: Now, open questions.

[20:09] Ben Carter: Question: Are we gating launch on an interactive page? Could we launch with PDF and add interactive later?

[20:17] Wei Chen: If we don’t have interactive, conversion might be lower.

[20:20] Ethan Wallace: Also press likes interactive.

[20:23] Jonas Weber: For credibility, interactive isn’t required. But it’s nice for transparency.

[20:30] Soojin Lee: We will plan for interactive as part of launch, but we can have a contingency: PDF + blog if web slips.

[20:39] Neha Kapoor: Please have contingency.

[20:42] (laughter)

[20:45] Rina Kobayashi: Another question: Do we include Dedicated and Private? Or just Hosted API? Because numbers differ.

[20:52] Soojin Lee: Good point. For v1, it’s easiest to benchmark Hosted because it’s consistent. But we should message “these optimizations also apply to Dedicated and Private” and Sales can use it as proof of runtime. We can add a section on how Dedicated/Private differ (isolation, residency, SLA).

[21:12] Mateo Alvarez: I agree. Benchmark Hosted, then position Dedicated/Private as deployment options with similar performance characteristics, but don’t publish those numbers unless we can standardize hardware.

[21:23] Claire Dubois: Yep.

[21:25] Ethan Wallace: For story, we can frame: “Same runtime across hosted/dedicated/private; benchmarks shown on hosted; ask us for dedicated sizing.”

[21:36] Ben Carter: That gives us enterprise CTA.

[21:39] Soojin Lee: Exactly.

[21:41] Tyler Benson: For attribution: we should define what counts as influenced pipeline. We’ll need campaign tag in HubSpot. I’ll file a request.

[21:49] Soojin Lee: Perfect.

[21:52] (short pause)

[21:54] Claire Dubois: One more technical detail: warmup. We should standardize: discard first N requests or run for X minutes before recording. Otherwise cold starts will bias.

[22:04] Jonas Weber: Yes. Warmup + steady state. And note that we are measuring steady state, not cold start. We can mention cold start separately.

[22:14] Neha Kapoor: And maybe note autoscaling effects. But that’s complicated.

[22:18] Mateo Alvarez: For hosted, autoscaling is part of reality. But for benchmark, we should fix capacity so queueing doesn’t dominate.

[22:27] Rina Kobayashi: Runner can pin capacity and then run a closed-loop load to keep concurrency constant.

[22:34] Claire Dubois: Great. Then we can attribute p95 to serving, not queue spikes.

[22:40] Soojin Lee: All right. Let’s close with concrete next steps and dates.

[22:45] Soojin Lee: Claire, you’ll circulate workload definition and prompt set plan by Friday.

[22:49] Claire Dubois: Yes.

[22:51] Soojin Lee: Rina, confirm runner + manifest fields, especially what we can publish.

[22:56] Rina Kobayashi: Yep.

[22:58] Soojin Lee: Vanessa, region capacity plan and proposed reservation windows.

[23:02] Vanessa Ortiz: Yep.

[23:04] Soojin Lee: Ethan, outline and figure list.

[23:07] Ethan Wallace: Yep, I’ll send a doc.

[23:09] Soojin Lee: Ben and Tyler, KPI plan and analytics events.

[23:13] Ben Carter: Yes.

[23:14] Tyler Benson: Yep.

[23:16] Soojin Lee: Wei and Elliot, draft methodology summary/disclaimers (v0) for the landing page.

[23:21] Wei Chen: Sounds good.

[23:22] Elliot Price: Yup.

[23:24] Soojin Lee: And I’ll schedule legal review and sales enablement review. Also I’ll set up a recurring benchmark standup Tue/Thu.

[23:33] Talia Benson: I’ll send calendar invite.

[23:36] Soojin Lee: Before we end—any blockers anyone wants to raise?

[23:41] Neha Kapoor: Just reiterating: launch traffic. Put SRE on the plan early. Also, if the interactive page pulls JSON, it needs caching and rate limiting.

[23:52] Mateo Alvarez: And from runtime: we’ll support but please avoid last-minute methodology changes.

[23:57] Claire Dubois: Understood.

[24:00] Soojin Lee: Great. Thanks all. I’ll send notes with owners and action items. Meeting adjourned.

[24:06] (Recording continues briefly; side chatter)

[24:08] Ethan Wallace: Jonas, can you sanity check the frontier chart idea with me?

[24:11] Jonas Weber: Yeah, ping me. Also we need to decide if we’re using p50 or p95 in that chart.

[24:15] (end)

Note: Fireflies transcript includes partial audio after the meeting ended; remaining recording was mostly silence and was truncated.

Summary (auto-generated):
- Team aligned on goals for a public benchmark report focused on production inference economics (latency per dollar) with reproducible methodology.

- Confirmed launch assets: interactive web table, downloadable PDF, blog post, sales enablement kit, webinar.

1) What we’re publishing + why now
2) Methodology and fairness (workloads, prompt sets, caching/batching)
3) Regions, capacity reservations, run logistics
4) Messaging, disclaimers, and competitive language
5) Launch plan + funnel instrumentation

Transcript:

[00:00] Soojin Lee: Alright, I think we’re at time. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the kickoff for the benchmark report—public-facing, focused on production inference economics, so like latency per dollar, not just raw tokens per second. Goal today is align on scope, owners, timeline, and like, what we will and won’t say.

[00:20] Talia Benson: Quick note, I’m recording and I’ll drop notes after. If anything sensitive comes up, call it out and we’ll flag for redaction.

[00:27] Ethan Wallace: Cool. And I’ll be annoying about narrative. Just warning everybody.

[00:31] Jonas Weber: Please be annoying, that’s helpful.

[00:34] Soojin Lee: Perfect. Let’s do a quick round of what each team needs out of this. I’ll start: PMM needs a credible, defensible story that’s consistent with what we can substantiate. Also Sales needs a talk track that doesn’t overclaim.

[00:48] Ben Carter: Growth perspective: we want this to drive signups and demo requests. So we need a landing page that converts, and instrumentation so we can attribute pipeline. Also, we should know what the “aha” is—like, what are we claiming Redwood is better at.

[01:02] Jonas Weber: Applied ML/perf side: we need methodology locked early. If we do “latency/$” it has to be well defined. Otherwise we’ll get roasted.

[01:11] Claire Dubois: Yup. And we need to make sure workloads reflect production distributions—prefill heavy vs decode heavy. Not just one synthetic prompt.

[01:18] Rina Kobayashi: From harness side: region sweeps are doable but capacity variability is real. We need reserved slots. Also we need to decide what artifacts are publishable—manifests, configs, etc.

[01:30] Mateo Alvarez: Runtime: we’re supportive, but I want to be clear: we are not going to ship kernel changes just to look good in a benchmark. Small guardrails or telemetry, fine. But not “benchmark-ware.”

[01:42] Neha Kapoor: Reliability: launch week traffic is a risk, plus we’ll get people hammering the page and maybe the API. So we need rate limit and caching plan.

[01:50] Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet: I need dates and region list. If you want NA, EU, APAC, I can try to reserve, but some regions are tight. We have to pick what’s realistic.

[02:01] Wei Chen: Docs/web copy: give me the disclaimers early. Also methodology summary needs to be readable, not a paper.

[02:09] Elliot Price: I’m here to help with the method write-up and make it consistent with how we talk about caching, batching, and what “typical” means.

[02:16] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s anchor on the “why now” and the deliverables. Ethan, can you summarize what’s shipping?

[02:24] Ethan Wallace: Sure. My current mental package is: 1) interactive table on the site—filter by model, region, context length maybe; 2) PDF report with narrative, charts, and a methodology section; 3) blog post that’s more digestible; 4) enablement kit—FAQ, talk track, maybe a one slide summary; and 5) webinar to walk through results and answer “is this fair.”

[02:47] Soojin Lee: Yes. And timeline wise, we’ve got like ~8 weeks in the strawman. We can adjust, but we should assume legal review and reviews will take time.

[03:00] Jonas Weber: Can I ask—are we planning any competitor baselines? Because that changes legal risk and methodology.

[03:06] Soojin Lee: Good question. Tentatively: we can include context, but we have to be cautious. We can say “here’s Redwood on X hardware with Y config.” If we do competitor numbers, it needs caveats and preferably from public sources or clearly stated measurement conditions.

[03:21] Ethan Wallace: I’d rather keep competitor references minimal in the first version. The story can be “this is what production economics look like; here’s how to evaluate.” But I know Sales will want comparison.

[03:34] Mateo Alvarez: Also competitor numbers are hard to make apples-to-apples. If someone runs AWS Bedrock with default settings vs our tuned runtime, people will argue.

[03:42] Claire Dubois: The Internet will argue regardless.

[03:44] (crosstalk)

[03:45] Ben Carter: If we don’t compare, do we still get pickup?

[03:48] Soojin Lee: We can still get pickup if the metric is novel and the methodology is strong. “Latency per dollar” and regional consistency is a good hook.

[03:56] Jonas Weber: On the technical side, we should publish enough artifacts so a skeptical engineer can reproduce. That’s the defense.

[04:06] Rina Kobayashi: +1, but we should plan redaction. Manifests can’t leak internal hostnames or request IDs.

[04:12] Wei Chen: And it has to be presented in a way that doesn’t scare off normal devs. Like “here’s the summary; here’s the full method if you want it.”

[04:21] Soojin Lee: Ok, let’s talk methodology. Claire, can you walk through what you think “latency/$” should be?

[04:28] Claire Dubois: Yeah. So, we need to define the unit: dollars per something and latency per something. Usually we can compute cost per 1M tokens given our pricing or underlying GPU cost for dedicated. But since this is public and spans hosted, maybe we compute using published hosted price per token. Then “latency/$” could be like (tokens per second) divided by ($ per second) effectively, but that gets confusing. Better: “time to generate a fixed output length” and “cost for that generation.” Then we can do “seconds per $” or invert to “outputs per $.”

[05:05] Jonas Weber: Right now internally we’ve been doing something like: latency per request (p50, p95) and cost per 1M tokens, and then a combined metric we call latency-per-dollar as a normalized score. But we need to define it precisely.

[05:18] Mateo Alvarez: Please no “score” that looks like we’re cooking numbers.

[05:22] Claire Dubois: Agree. We should avoid a single magic score unless it’s literally a ratio with clear units.

[05:28] Ben Carter: Marketing-wise, units are good. “p95 latency at $X per 1M tokens” is understandable.

[05:35] Ethan Wallace: Could be a table that toggles: show p50/p95 latency, throughput, and cost, then let people decide. Then we highlight a couple insights.

[05:46] Soojin Lee: Ok. Key methodology questions that keep coming up: caching/batching, concurrency, prompt sets, and warmups. Jonas, where are we right now?

[05:56] Jonas Weber: For a production-like benchmark, we should enable the things customers actually use. So continuous batching is on. For caching, it depends. Prefix caching can dramatically change economics for repeated prompts. If we enable it, we need to say under what cache hit rate. If we disable it, people will say we’re not showing real world savings.

[06:20] Elliot Price: I’ve seen this go sideways. We should separate: baseline run with cache off (to show raw serving performance) and an optional scenario with cache on with a defined synthetic hit rate or a defined workload that naturally has repeats.

[06:33] Claire Dubois: Another option: keep caching policy fixed—like “KV cache reuse off; standard attention KV cache obviously exists,” but no prefix reuse across requests. Then we can have a section: “if your workload has repeats, caching can improve cost/latency; here’s how to estimate.”

[06:51] Rina Kobayashi: That’s easiest to implement and defend. If we do cross-request caching, we need to model user behavior.

[06:58] Ben Carter: From funnel perspective, I’d rather not bury the lead in nuance, but I get it.

[07:05] Soojin Lee: Legal will also prefer less “it depends” but we have to be honest. We can message: “Benchmark uses conservative assumptions; your workload may see better results with caching.”

[07:17] Mateo Alvarez: Also, concurrency. If we run at low concurrency, we’ll look good on p50 but not throughput. If we run at high concurrency, we might degrade p95. We should pick something realistic.

[07:30] Claire Dubois: I propose we define multiple load points: concurrency 1 (single user), concurrency 8/16 (small production), and maybe 64 for stress. But that’s a lot of runs.

[07:43] Vanessa Ortiz: That is a lot of GPU hours.

[07:46] Jonas Weber: We can limit to two concurrencies: one “interactive” and one “service” mode. E.g., C=1 and C=16.

[07:55] Neha Kapoor: And we should run repeats across time windows to catch variance. Otherwise a region with a noisy neighbor day will look bad.

[08:05] Rina Kobayashi: We can add stability gates. Like if p95 variance > threshold across 3 runs, auto-rerun.

[08:13] Mateo Alvarez: That’s fine. But it adds time.

[08:16] Soojin Lee: Better time than a PR crisis.

[08:19] (laughter)

[08:22] Ethan Wallace: On prompt sets, what’s the plan? Because for narrative I need to say “we used a production mix.”

[08:29] Claire Dubois: We have an internal prompt set v2, but it’s a bit too “eval-y.” I want to update to something like v3 that includes: short chat turns, long context summarization, RAG-ish prompts, and structured output. Also include prefill-heavy tasks and decode-heavy tasks.

[08:50] Jonas Weber: And we should publish the categories and distributions, not necessarily the raw prompts if there’s any IP concerns.

[08:58] Wei Chen: If we don’t publish prompts, people may say it’s not reproducible.

[09:03] Elliot Price: We can publish hashed prompts or a synthetic open set. Or publish the generator code and category definitions.

[09:10] Rina Kobayashi: We can publish prompt IDs and lengths, and a small sample set. Full set maybe in a GitHub repo with review.

[09:20] Soojin Lee: Let’s park exact publication details, but align that we need reproducibility artifacts.

[09:26] Tyler Benson: Can I jump in on measurement outputs? For the web page, we need a stable schema. Model, region, context length bucket, concurrency, p50, p95, throughput, cost per 1M tokens, and a timestamp / run id. Also needs “methodology version.”

[09:44] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, we can do JSON + CSV export. But please decide early what dimensions we’re exposing because it affects data volume.

[09:53] Ben Carter: Also, for the interactive page, we want filters but not 20 knobs. Too many knobs hurts conversion.

[10:01] Ethan Wallace: Agree. We can default to the “recommended” workload and let advanced users toggle.

[10:06] Soojin Lee: Ok. Regions and capacity. Vanessa, can you outline constraints?

[10:12] Vanessa Ortiz: Sure. For NA we’re good: us-east and us-west are fine. For EU, eu-west is usually okay but we have some reserved commitments. APAC is the tricky one. ap-southeast sometimes has limited availability depending on instance type. If we want consistent results, we should reserve blocks and run all models in a tight window.

[10:34] Neha Kapoor: We also need to avoid running during known maintenance windows.

[10:38] Vanessa Ortiz: Right. And we need to choose instance types—like are we standardizing on one GPU class or are we “best available per region”? Because that affects fairness.

[10:50] Mateo Alvarez: For hosted, we control the fleet but it’s not always identical across regions. For a benchmark, maybe we pin to a specific GPU for all runs, even if it’s not our default in one region.

[11:03] Jonas Weber: If we change fleet config just for the benchmark, it’s not representative.

[11:08] Mateo Alvarez: Not change permanently, but reserve identical slices.

[11:12] Soojin Lee: This is one of those tradeoffs. We want representative but also comparable. Maybe we present “this is what customers see in region X as-of date,” and we list the hardware. That’s honest.

[11:28] Claire Dubois: Yes. Publish the hardware profile per region. Then no one can say we hid it.

[11:34] Ethan Wallace: But does that create confusion? “Why is region A faster? Because different GPUs.”

[11:40] Ben Carter: That might be okay. People care about what they will experience.

[11:45] Wei Chen: If we do that, we need a tooltip that explains “hardware may differ by region.”

[11:51] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s shift to messaging and claims. I know Legal isn’t on this call, but we should plan the review. The big risk: “fastest” claims. We should avoid absolutes unless we can substantiate across all workloads.

[12:09] Ethan Wallace: So the core narrative could be: 1) production inference economics are multi-dimensional; 2) Redwood provides a consistent, optimized runtime; 3) here’s what we measured and how; 4) key insights: regional consistency, p95 stability, and cost efficiency.

[12:28] Jonas Weber: Also include reliability. Not just speed. People care about tail latency.

[12:33] Neha Kapoor: Yes please. p95 and error rates. We can report 5xx/429 rates during runs maybe, or at least note we excluded failed runs.

[12:42] Claire Dubois: If we include error rates, we need to define exclusion criteria. Like if a run has more than X% errors, discard and rerun.

[12:50] Rina Kobayashi: We already have rerun logic we can add. Or manual gates.

[12:55] Soojin Lee: Ok. On competitive, we can say “compared to common managed endpoints, you should evaluate with these factors.” But we shouldn’t say “2x faster than OpenAI” unless we have very strong evidence and permission.

[13:10] Ben Carter: Can we at least mention “AWS Bedrock” as an example of “general managed endpoints”?

[13:15] Soojin Lee: Mentioning as category is okay. Specific numerical comparisons are where we need legal sign-off.

[13:22] Wei Chen: For disclaimers, we should include: results depend on workload, concurrency, region, time of day, and model version. Also “as of date.”

[13:34] Elliot Price: And “we used Redwood-optimized configs.” People will ask if they can get the same. We should say these settings are available to customers (batching, etc.) and we’ll link to docs.

[13:46] Mateo Alvarez: As long as that’s true. Some configs might be internal defaults.

[13:50] Jonas Weber: We should only publish numbers for configurations that customers can actually select or that are default behavior.

[13:57] Soojin Lee: Great constraint: publish only supported configs.

[14:01] Talia Benson: I’m noting: “supported vs experimental” needs definition. We should align with Product.

[14:07] Soojin Lee: Yes, Henry isn’t here but we’ll pull him in for model/region “supported” list.

[14:14] Ben Carter: On the funnel, I want to propose CTAs per segment. For self-serve, “Get API key + credits.” For enterprise, “Talk to us about Dedicated/Private.” The benchmark page should route to both.

[14:28] Ethan Wallace: And we need a “how we measured” explainer that’s short, so people don’t bounce.

[14:33] Wei Chen: I can draft that as a collapsible section.

[14:36] Tyler Benson: Analytics events I’ll need: filter changes, export clicks, PDF download, CTA clicks, time on page, and scroll depth maybe. Also UTM capture.

[14:49] Soojin Lee: Great. Let’s discuss timeline. Strawman: next 2 weeks we lock methodology and implement any required instrumentation, weeks 3-5 run sweeps, week 6 content, week 7 reviews, week 8 launch. Is that feasible?

[15:07] Rina Kobayashi: Feasible if we don’t have a million workload variants.

[15:10] Vanessa Ortiz: Feasible if you give me region list this week.

[15:14] Jonas Weber: Feasible if methodology is frozen by end of next week. Otherwise it slips.

[15:20] Ethan Wallace: I need early data to draft charts. Even partial results.

[15:25] Claire Dubois: We can do an early “pilot sweep” with 2 models, 2 regions, to validate harness and produce initial charts.

[15:34] Mateo Alvarez: Good. That will also reveal runtime regressions.

[15:39] Neha Kapoor: And it helps SRE plan for launch week traffic—what endpoints, caching, etc.

[15:45] Soojin Lee: Ok, let’s list decisions we need this week.

[15:49] (pause)

[15:51] Soojin Lee: 1) Model shortlist. Jonas/Claire, can you propose? Keep it curated.

[15:58] Jonas Weber: We should include at least one small, one mid, one large model. Maybe a couple popular open weights. We should also consider embeddings? Or is it just generation?

[16:09] Ethan Wallace: For narrative, keep it to generation in v1, or it becomes too big.

[16:14] Ben Carter: Agree. Embeddings is a separate report.

[16:18] Claire Dubois: Ok, generation only. Proposed: a 7–8B class, a 13–14B class, a 70B-ish, and maybe one MoE if we support it. And we should pin model versions.

[16:33] Mateo Alvarez: Make sure they’re actually stable in production.

[16:36] Soojin Lee: 2) Region list: likely us-east, us-west, eu-west, and one APAC. Vanessa will advise.

[16:44] Vanessa Ortiz: I’d say ap-southeast if possible, or ap-northeast depending on capacity. I’ll confirm.

[16:51] Soojin Lee: 3) Workloads: at least two context length buckets, and two concurrencies. Claire to propose.

[16:58] Claire Dubois: Yep.

[17:00] Soojin Lee: 4) Caching stance: baseline without cross-request prefix caching; continuous batching on; publish that.

[17:08] Jonas Weber: Works. We should be explicit: we’re not modeling cache hit rates in the primary metric.

[17:14] Elliot Price: And we can add a sidebar: “If you have repeated prefixes, here’s how caching changes the math.”

[17:20] Soojin Lee: Great.

[17:22] (audio glitch)

[17:25] Ben Carter: One more: naming. Are we calling it “latency per dollar” or “latency/$” or “throughput per dollar”?

[17:33] Ethan Wallace: “Latency per dollar” is catchy but could be misunderstood. “Better latency at lower cost” is safer.

[17:41] Soojin Lee: We’ll test terms. But for now internally call it latency/$.

[17:47] Tyler Benson: If we’re using a combined metric, we need to decide if it’s ratio or two-axis plots.

[17:53] Jonas Weber: I’d prefer a frontier chart: latency vs cost. Then we can say “efficient frontier.”

[18:01] Ethan Wallace: Frontier chart is cool but maybe too nerdy. We can do both—frontier in PDF, table in web.

[18:10] Wei Chen: On web, keep it simple. Table + a couple charts.

[18:16] Soojin Lee: Ok. Let’s talk objections we anticipate. This will inform FAQ. Markus isn’t here, but we should seed it. What will engineers complain about?

[18:27] Jonas Weber: “Your workload isn’t my workload.”

[18:30] Claire Dubois: “You cherry-picked prompts.”

[18:33] Mateo Alvarez: “You tuned your runtime but not others.”

[18:36] Neha Kapoor: “Regions vary day to day.”

[18:39] Rina Kobayashi: “You hid errors and retries.”

[18:42] Ben Carter: “This doesn’t match what I see.”

[18:45] Soojin Lee: Great. So we need: clear methodology, versioning, run dates, hardware, and stability notes.

[18:55] Elliot Price: Also define how we treat streaming. Are we measuring time-to-first-token and time-to-last-token? People care.

[19:02] Claire Dubois: We should capture TTFT and TPOT maybe. But that multiplies metrics.

[19:09] Jonas Weber: At minimum we should report TTFT and end-to-end latency for a fixed token output. TTFT is key for chat.

[19:18] Mateo Alvarez: Streaming instrumentation exists; Omar can help, but not in this call.

[19:24] Soojin Lee: Let’s include TTFT + end-to-end p95 as core.

[19:29] Wei Chen: If we add TTFT, we need definitions on the page or people will misread.

[19:34] Ethan Wallace: That’s fine. We can do tooltips.

[19:38] Soojin Lee: Ok, I want to make sure we have clear owners. Jonas/Claire/Rina own benchmark design and data. Mateo/Neha/Vanessa own runtime+infra support and capacity. Ethan owns narrative. Ben+Tyler own funnel and analytics. Wei+Elliot own docs/methodology summary. I own the launch plan, message house, and sales enablement.

[20:04] Talia Benson: Captured.

[20:06] Soojin Lee: Now, open questions.

[20:09] Ben Carter: Question: Are we gating launch on an interactive page? Could we launch with PDF and add interactive later?

[20:17] Wei Chen: If we don’t have interactive, conversion might be lower.

[20:20] Ethan Wallace: Also press likes interactive.

[20:23] Jonas Weber: For credibility, interactive isn’t required. But it’s nice for transparency.

[20:30] Soojin Lee: We will plan for interactive as part of launch, but we can have a contingency: PDF + blog if web slips.

[20:39] Neha Kapoor: Please have contingency.

[20:42] (laughter)

[20:45] Rina Kobayashi: Another question: Do we include Dedicated and Private? Or just Hosted API? Because numbers differ.

[20:52] Soojin Lee: Good point. For v1, it’s easiest to benchmark Hosted because it’s consistent. But we should message “these optimizations also apply to Dedicated and Private” and Sales can use it as proof of runtime. We can add a section on how Dedicated/Private differ (isolation, residency, SLA).

[21:12] Mateo Alvarez: I agree. Benchmark Hosted, then position Dedicated/Private as deployment options with similar performance characteristics, but don’t publish those numbers unless we can standardize hardware.

[21:23] Claire Dubois: Yep.

[21:25] Ethan Wallace: For story, we can frame: “Same runtime across hosted/dedicated/private; benchmarks shown on hosted; ask us for dedicated sizing.”

[21:36] Ben Carter: That gives us enterprise CTA.

[21:39] Soojin Lee: Exactly.

[21:41] Tyler Benson: For attribution: we should define what counts as influenced pipeline. We’ll need campaign tag in HubSpot. I’ll file a request.

[21:49] Soojin Lee: Perfect.

[21:52] (short pause)

[21:54] Claire Dubois: One more technical detail: warmup. We should standardize: discard first N requests or run for X minutes before recording. Otherwise cold starts will bias.

[22:04] Jonas Weber: Yes. Warmup + steady state. And note that we are measuring steady state, not cold start. We can mention cold start separately.

[22:14] Neha Kapoor: And maybe note autoscaling effects. But that’s complicated.

[22:18] Mateo Alvarez: For hosted, autoscaling is part of reality. But for benchmark, we should fix capacity so queueing doesn’t dominate.

[22:27] Rina Kobayashi: Runner can pin capacity and then run a closed-loop load to keep concurrency constant.

[22:34] Claire Dubois: Great. Then we can attribute p95 to serving, not queue spikes.

[22:40] Soojin Lee: All right. Let’s close with concrete next steps and dates.

[22:45] Soojin Lee: Claire, you’ll circulate workload definition and prompt set plan by Friday.

[22:49] Claire Dubois: Yes.

[22:51] Soojin Lee: Rina, confirm runner + manifest fields, especially what we can publish.

[22:56] Rina Kobayashi: Yep.

[22:58] Soojin Lee: Vanessa, region capacity plan and proposed reservation windows.

[23:02] Vanessa Ortiz: Yep.

[23:04] Soojin Lee: Ethan, outline and figure list.

[23:07] Ethan Wallace: Yep, I’ll send a doc.

[23:09] Soojin Lee: Ben and Tyler, KPI plan and analytics events.

[23:13] Ben Carter: Yes.

[23:14] Tyler Benson: Yep.

[23:16] Soojin Lee: Wei and Elliot, draft methodology summary/disclaimers (v0) for the landing page.

[23:21] Wei Chen: Sounds good.

[23:22] Elliot Price: Yup.

[23:24] Soojin Lee: And I’ll schedule legal review and sales enablement review. Also I’ll set up a recurring benchmark standup Tue/Thu.

[23:33] Talia Benson: I’ll send calendar invite.

[23:36] Soojin Lee: Before we end—any blockers anyone wants to raise?

[23:41] Neha Kapoor: Just reiterating: launch traffic. Put SRE on the plan early. Also, if the interactive page pulls JSON, it needs caching and rate limiting.

[23:52] Mateo Alvarez: And from runtime: we’ll support but please avoid last-minute methodology changes.

[23:57] Claire Dubois: Understood.

[24:00] Soojin Lee: Great. Thanks all. I’ll send notes with owners and action items. Meeting adjourned.

[24:06] (Recording continues briefly; side chatter)

[24:08] Ethan Wallace: Jonas, can you sanity check the frontier chart idea with me?

[24:11] Jonas Weber: Yeah, ping me. Also we need to decide if we’re using p50 or p95 in that chart.

[24:15] (end)

Note: Fireflies transcript includes partial audio after the meeting ended; remaining recording was mostly silence and was truncated.

title: Internal: Runtime LTS pilot kickoff (scope, owners, timelines)
preview:
Meeting: Internal: Runtime LTS pilot kickoff (scope, owners, timelines)
Recorded: 2025-03-12 10:02am PT
Duration: 55 min
Attendees: Connor O'Brien, Talia Benson, Mateo Alvarez, Noah Patel, Hiro Tanaka, Sofia Ivanova, Sahana Iyer, Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant, Marissa Cole, Rachel Kim, Dev Patel

Auto-Summary (generated)
- Purpose: align cross-functional owners on the Runtime LTS pilot scope, success criteria, patch cadence, and release readiness gates.
- Decisions in-progress: pilot will start on runtime-1.18-lts baseline, with monthly patch cadence and out-of-band security patches; strict perf regression gates required; Support intake will drive backport requests with a risk rubric.
- Key risks: perf regressions, backport correctness risk, CI capacity for benchmarks, and customer comms around “mandatory” vs “optional” patches.

Topics
1) Pilot scope and target customers (Dedicated + Private)
2) Branch/versioning and customer pinning semantics
3) Backport policy + approvals + test/perf gates
4) Artifact provenance (checksums/signing) and reproducible builds
5) SRE escalation, SLO dashboards, and patch rollout monitoring
6) Release notes taxonomy (“slower-change”) and Console/Docs surface area

Questions captured
- What exactly makes a patch “mandatory” for LTS customers vs recommended?
- How do we handle noisy perf benchmarks (rerun vs waiver) without eroding trust?
- For Private: what is the minimum viable version pinning story during pilot (installer + Helm)?

Transcript

[00:00] Connor O'Brien: Alright, I think we’re good to start. This is the Runtime LTS pilot kickoff. Goal today is not to re-litigate the entire ADR, but to get, like, owners and a crisp pilot plan: scope, timelines, gates, and comms. Talia’s driving notes.

[00:18] Talia Benson: Yep, I’ve got notes. If folks can say your name the first time you speak, it helps the transcript. Fireflies is… it’s fine.

[00:28] Mateo Alvarez: Mateo here. Just to set tone: LTS is for enterprise trust. We cannot ship something called LTS and then ask them to upgrade every two weeks because we broke latency. So the pilot has to prove the process.

[00:43] Connor O'Brien: Exactly. So quick framing: LTS channel for serving runtime. The baseline branch is runtime-1.18-lts. We want a 6 to 12+ month support window, monthly patches, out-of-band security fixes as needed. Pilot is 1 to 3 enterprise accounts, Dedicated and/or Private. Rachel’s going to help pick accounts.

[01:06] Rachel Kim: Rachel, CS. Yeah, I can share a shortlist. Some want Private with change-control windows, like, once a month, sometimes quarterly. So the cadence needs to map to their process.

[01:19] Marissa Cole: Marissa, Product. And from product side: we need to be consistent about what customers will see in Console and docs. “LTS” label, pinning, and release notes that don’t read like mainline.

[01:32] Sean Gallagher: Sean, SRE. The other dimension is operational ownership. If we’re doing LTS, there needs to be an escalation path for Sev1s and clarity on whether we’re on the hook for a same-day hotfix versus next patch.

[01:45] Connor O'Brien: Great. Let’s go top-down. Pilot scope: what’s included and what isn’t.

[01:52] Talia Benson: I’ll prompt: runtime only, right? Not full platform.

[01:56] Connor O'Brien: Correct. The runtime artifacts and packaging for Dedicated and Private. We’re not forking architecture. We’re not promising feature parity with main. We’re promising stability, predictable patching, and audited artifacts.

[02:11] Noah Patel: Noah, runtime architecture. Just to be explicit: “close to mainline” means backports are cherry-picks with minimal diff. No giant refactors.

[02:20] Hiro Tanaka: Hiro, kernels. Also kernel autotune. On main we change defaults frequently. On LTS we should freeze defaults and only move them if it’s fixing a correctness issue or a clear Sev-level incident.

[02:33] Sofia Ivanova: Sofia, scheduling. Same theme. We’ve had batching heuristics that are… good on average, but they create variance tails. LTS should prefer predictable p95 over squeezing throughput.

[02:46] Connor O'Brien: Yup, that’s the philosophy. Now, branch/versioning. We’re proposing runtime-1.18-lts as the branch. Tags like 1.18.1-lts, 1.18.2-lts, etc. Does anyone have a strong objection?

[03:02] Sahana Iyer: Sahana, CI/build. No objection, but it changes CI and artifact publishing paths. Release branches need their own workflow so we’re not leaking secrets or signing keys to PRs from random forks.

[03:16] Connor O'Brien: Totally. We’ll talk gates and signing in a minute.

[03:20] Marissa Cole: Product question: do we expect customers to pin to “LTS channel latest” or to an exact patch? Because Support is going to ask.

[03:28] Connor O'Brien: For enterprise, we should support exact patch pinning. “Latest” is nice for hosted but Private customers want deterministic upgrades.

[03:38] Rachel Kim: +1. In regulated change control, “latest” is not a thing. They need a known version with release notes.

[03:45] Sean Gallagher: Also for incident response: you need to know what exact bits are running.

[03:52] Connor O'Brien: Good. So: patch pinning is the default story for pilot. If Console doesn’t fully support it yet, we’ll provide manual instructions and links.

[04:05] Marissa Cole: That’s acceptable for pilot, as long as we don’t oversell it.

[04:09] Talia Benson: I’m capturing: “pin to exact patch; channel selection optional/limited during pilot.”

[04:15] Connor O'Brien: Next: backport policy. Noah, do you want to walk through proposed eligibility?

[04:22] Noah Patel: Sure. Proposed rubric: eligible items are (1) security fixes, (2) Sev1 production incidents like crashes/data corruption, (3) Sev2 issues with clear customer impact where the fix is small and low-risk, and (4) compliance-related build/provenance fixes if required by customers. Not eligible: features, performance improvements that alter behavior, or refactors. Any exceptions require Mateo + Release Eng + SRE sign-off.

[04:53] Dev Patel: Dev, Support. Can we translate that to something Support can triage? Like, if customer says “latency got worse,” is that Sev2? Or is that just, “sorry”?

[05:04] Sean Gallagher: It depends. If they’re violating SLO we sold them, it’s Sev1/Sev2. If it’s “my benchmark is 3% slower” it’s not automatically backport worthy.

[05:14] Allison Grant: Allison, observability/perf. This is why we need perf gates and a baseline. We should define acceptable variance, otherwise every patch debate becomes vibes.

[05:25] Connor O'Brien: Exactly. Marcus isn’t here, but we’ll align on perf gate thresholds. Allison, can you propose something? p95 regression thresholds, throughput regression thresholds.

[05:34] Allison Grant: Yes. For pilot, I’d recommend we gate on canonical workloads: mixed prompt lengths, streaming chat, and a long-context case. GPU coverage: A100 and H100 at minimum; maybe L40S if it’s in Dedicated pools. Threshold-wise: p50 within +/- 2%, p95 within +/- 3% on latency, throughput within -2% unless there’s a known tradeoff. But we need to talk about noise and reruns.

[06:03] Sahana Iyer: Noise is real. If we run perf canaries on shared runners, you’ll see random 4% swings and we’ll block releases forever.

[06:12] Sean Gallagher: So we need a waiver process that’s explicit. “Rerun twice; if still failing, SRE + Release Eng can sign waiver with justification and link to graphs.” Otherwise it becomes “we waived it because we wanted to ship.”

[06:27] Connor O'Brien: Agree. Talia, capture: perf gate with rerun policy + waiver policy.

[06:32] Talia Benson: Got it.

[06:34] Hiro Tanaka: One more thing: kernel changes can look “small” but have huge perf implications. On LTS, kernel autotune params should be pinned. And if we have to change them, it must be called out in release notes, like, “expect +/- X” and how to override.

[06:50] Connor O'Brien: Yes. That’s part of “slower-change release notes.”

[06:55] Marissa Cole: On that, release notes: enterprise expects a compatibility matrix and known issues section. If we just dump PR titles, they’ll ignore it.

[07:07] Connor O'Brien: We’re implementing an LTS mode for the release notes generator that forces categories: Security, Stability/Correctness, Performance, Operational/Packaging, Known issues, Upgrade notes, Compatibility. Each patch needs metadata.

[07:25] Dev Patel: Also need support-facing “what changed” and “what should I ask the customer.” Like, what logs to gather if they say “scheduler is stalling.”

[07:33] Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling. Can we add a targeted scheduler regression suite on release branches? Mixed traffic, backpressure, overload. That’s where we’ve been burned.

[07:43] Sahana Iyer: We can. It’s more CI runtime though. We might run it nightly and on release candidates rather than every PR.

[07:51] Connor O'Brien: For LTS patches, we can afford heavier gates. We’re shipping monthly. It’s okay if RC takes a day.

[08:00] Mateo Alvarez: Right, and we should say that explicitly: LTS patch week is a process. It’s not “merge and tag.”

[08:06] Talia Benson: I’ll put that in the plan.

[08:10] Connor O'Brien: Artifact provenance: checksums, signing, reproducible builds. Sahana, what’s realistic for pilot?

[08:18] Sahana Iyer: For pilot: we can generate SHA256 checksums for runtime images and the Private installer artifacts, publish them alongside release notes, and sign them with our internal signing key. Reproducible builds… we can get close with pinned toolchains and containerized builds, but true byte-for-byte reproducibility across environments might take longer. We can at least provide build provenance metadata: git commit, build ID, builder image digest.

[08:47] Sean Gallagher: For Private customers, the big win is “I can verify the artifact I downloaded matches what Redwood published.” Checksums + signature gets you a lot.

[08:56] Marissa Cole: And it needs to be documented in a way their security team can follow. Like, command lines, where the public key is.

[09:04] Connor O'Brien: Yes. For pilot we’ll provide a verification doc and include it in the release notes appendix.

[09:11] Rachel Kim: Also, their auditors sometimes ask for SBOM. Do we have that?

[09:16] Sahana Iyer: SBOM generation is in flight. For pilot, we might have a “best effort” SBOM, or at least a dependency manifest. But we should be careful with commitments.

[09:27] Mateo Alvarez: Don’t promise SBOM if we can’t deliver consistently. But we can say “SBOM planned; available on request for pilot accounts.”

[09:34] Connor O'Brien: Agreed. 

[09:36] Connor O'Brien: Next: SRE ownership and escalation. Sean?

[09:41] Sean Gallagher: For pilot, I want a clear escalation matrix. If a pilot customer hits a Sev1 (crash, wide outage, major latency regression that breaches SLO), we page runtime on-call and SRE, and we decide within, say, 24 hours whether we (a) backport and cut an out-of-band patch, or (b) queue for monthly. For security, we’ll do out-of-band with expedited approvals.

[10:10] Dev Patel: Support needs language. Like, “we will have a patch within X days.” Otherwise customers panic.

[10:16] Connor O'Brien: That’s part of the security patch SLA ticket. For pilot, we can set expectations: security out-of-band “as soon as validated,” and stability Sev1 out-of-band if there’s no workaround.

[10:31] Allison Grant: Also: mandatory vs optional patches. If it’s security or Sev1, it’s mandatory. If it’s a Sev2 fix that doesn’t apply to them, it’s optional.

[10:41] Marissa Cole: But how do we communicate “mandatory”? In Console? Email? Support note?

[10:46] Rachel Kim: For pilot accounts, we can do direct comms. A patch advisory email from CS + Support, with a clear subject line.

[10:55] Connor O'Brien: For pilot, yes, direct comms is fine. For GA, we’ll have a broader channel.

[11:03] Talia Benson: Capturing: “pilot comms via CS email + Support macro; mandatory flag based on severity categories.”

[11:12] Connor O'Brien: Next: timeline. We said pilot starts after we cut first patch from runtime-1.18-lts. We’re targeting first LTS patch within ~4 weeks. Is that crazy?

[11:24] Sahana Iyer: Depends on CI capacity. If perf runners aren’t provisioned, yes it’s crazy.

[11:30] Sean Gallagher: We can prioritize capacity. But we need a plan so perf gates don’t starve other workloads.

[11:37] Allison Grant: I can work with Jonas/Claire on methodology so we don’t burn cycles on noisy tests.

[11:45] Connor O'Brien: Okay. For pilot: 
- Week 1: finalize branch, labeling, backport rubric, release notes template.
- Week 2-3: CI/perf gate integration + artifact signing/checksums.
- Week 4: cut 1.18.1-lts, publish notes, roll to pilot accounts.

[12:10] Mateo Alvarez: That’s aggressive but doable. The pilot is the forcing function.

[12:16] Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling tests might slip. But we can at least have smoke tests: queueing/backpressure.

[12:22] Connor O'Brien: Yes, minimum viable tests for first patch. Add more in subsequent.

[12:28] Marissa Cole: On Console: for pilot we may not have full UI for “choose LTS,” but we can have docs and a release notes page. For Dedicated, we can coordinate through account configs. For Private, it’s the installer/Helm values.

[12:45] Connor O'Brien: Works.

[12:47] Dev Patel: Support question: where do backport requests go? Linear ticket? Jira? Slack?

[12:53] Connor O'Brien: For pilot, intake starts in Support ticket (Jira) with a template. Dev owns triage. If it qualifies, we open a Linear task under ENG-4821 with a link and tag it “backport-candidate.” Release Eng then schedules it for next patch or OOB.

[13:12] Dev Patel: Okay, but we need required fields: runtime version, model, workload, repro steps, logs, business impact.

[13:20] Sean Gallagher: And severity mapping. “Customer can’t serve traffic” is Sev1. “Increased p95 but within SLO” maybe Sev3.

[13:28] Talia Benson: I’ll work with Dev/Rachel on the template.

[13:32] Hiro Tanaka: Also, if the issue is perf variance due to autotune, we might have a workaround: pin an env var or disable a kernel path. That can be documented as known issue instead of backport.

[13:46] Connor O'Brien: Yes. The known issues doc is living and linked from release notes.

[13:52] Allison Grant: And we should track “workaround only” vs “planned backport.” Customers care.

[13:57] Marissa Cole: Release notes should have: “Known issues (new), Known issues (still present), Resolved issues.”

[14:05] Connor O'Brien: Great suggestion.

[14:23] Connor O'Brien: Agreed.
